title.en: Vertex AI: Model Garden & the Gemini APIs

description.en: Tour Vertex AI's unified ML platform, choose between Gemini tiers and Model Garden's open and partner models, and learn the token/context budgeting every LLM app needs.

story_variants.en:
## One Platform, Every Model Lifecycle

**Vertex AI** is Google Cloud's unified machine learning platform: datasets, training, tuning, a model registry, endpoints, pipelines, monitoring, and — since the generative wave — the enterprise front door to **Gemini** and hundreds of other foundation models. The unifying idea is that whether you trained a scikit-learn model yourself or you're calling a frontier LLM, the surrounding lifecycle (versioning, serving, governance, observability) should look the same.

This lesson covers the generative entry points; the next two cover training and serving for models you build.

## Model Garden: The Catalog

**Model Garden** is a curated catalog of deployable models in three broad groups:

- **Google models** — the Gemini family (multimodal LLMs), Imagen (image generation/editing), Veo (video), Chirp (speech), plus embedding models for text and multimodal content.
- **Partner models** — third-party frontier models such as Anthropic's Claude, offered *as a service* on GCP infrastructure with GCP billing and data-use terms (Model-as-a-Service: no infrastructure for you to manage).
- **Open models** — Gemma, Llama, Mistral and friends, which you can one-click deploy onto Vertex endpoints with GPUs you control, or download and self-host.

> **Callout:** The enterprise reason Model Garden matters isn't variety — it's *procurement and governance*. One contract, one IAM system, one audit log, one quota page for every model your teams use. Shadow-AI API keys to five vendors is precisely what it replaces.

## The Gemini Tiers: A Cost-Quality Dial

Gemini ships as a family, refreshed regularly (2.5-era at the time of writing): **Pro** for maximum reasoning quality, **Flash** as the workhorse balancing cost and capability, and **Flash-Lite** for high-volume, latency-sensitive tasks like classification and extraction. All are natively multimodal (text, images, audio, video, PDFs in; text and increasingly other modalities out) and offer context windows up to around a million tokens.

Decision guidance that survives model renames:

| Question | If yes... |
|---|---|
| Is the task extraction/classification/simple routing? | Smallest tier; measure before upgrading |
| Does it need multi-step reasoning, coding, or tool use? | Workhorse tier with thinking budgets, escalate on evals |
| Is it customer-visible synthesis of high-stakes content? | Top tier plus grounding (lesson 12) |
| Are you calling it a million times a day? | Tier down, add caching, batch where possible |

Pricing is per token, input and output priced separately, with **context caching** (pay once for a long shared prefix like a policy manual) and **batch prediction** (asynchronous, discounted) as the two structural cost levers.

## Calling Gemini Like an Engineer

The modern SDK is the **Google Gen AI SDK** (`google-genai`), which targets both the Vertex AI API (service-account auth, enterprise controls, provisioned throughput) and the consumer Gemini API (API key) with the same code:

- `generate_content` for single-shot and multimodal calls; `chats` for stateful conversations.
- **System instructions** set persona and rules; **safety settings** tune blocking thresholds per harm category.
- **Structured output**: pass a JSON schema and the model must return conforming JSON — the difference between a demo and a parseable pipeline.
- **Function calling / tools**: declare functions; the model returns a structured call for your code to execute.

> **Pitfall:** The most common production failure isn't model quality — it's context overflow. Teams concatenate retrieved documents until requests exceed the window or the output budget, and truncation silently eats the final instruction. Budget tokens like memory: explicitly.

## Token Budgeting: The Skill Behind Every LLM App

Everything you send and receive is measured in **tokens** (roughly four characters of English text each; countable precisely via the SDK's `count_tokens`). A request must fit: system instruction + conversation history + retrieved context + the user's question + *reserved space for the answer*. The reservation matters — `max_output_tokens` you don't leave room for is an answer that stops mid-sentence.

So real applications run a packing loop: fixed costs first (system prompt, output reservation), then greedily admit context documents while they fit, skipping those that don't. That loop is this lesson's challenge, and it reappears in lesson 12 as the final stage of every RAG pipeline.

## Enterprise Grade Means Boring Guarantees

What distinguishes calling Gemini via Vertex from a hobby key: VPC Service Controls perimeters, CMEK encryption options, data-residency commitments, IAM-scoped access (`roles/aiplatform.user`), per-model quota you can see and raise, audit logging of every call, and **Provisioned Throughput** when you need guaranteed capacity instead of shared-pool best effort. None of it changes your prompt; all of it changes whether security signs off.

## What You'll Build

A context-window packer: given a budget, an output reservation, a system prompt, and four candidate documents, decide inclusion greedily and report the final token utilization. Deterministic, testable, and the exact shape of the function sitting in front of every production Gemini call you'll ever write.

description.en: One platform wraps both models you build and foundation models you call.

left.label: Route every request to the top-tier model 'to be safe'

right.label: Match tier to task; escalate only when evals demand it

description.en: The cost-quality dial is meant to be turned — per use case, with measurements.

animation_sequence.code:
from google import genai
client = genai.Client(vertexai=True, project="pym", location="us-central1")
resp = client.models.generate_content(
    model="gemini-2.5-flash",
    contents="Summarize this incident report...")
print(resp.text)

explanation.en: vertexai=True routes through Vertex: service-account auth, audit logs, enterprise quotas.
Model tier is a string — swapping Flash for Pro is a one-line eval experiment.
contents accepts text, images, audio, video, or PDFs — Gemini is natively multimodal.

description.en: Budgeting output the packer in your challenge produces.
The context-packing loop in front of every production LLM call.

instruction.en: Build a Gemini context-window packer. Context window = 8000 tokens; reserve 1000 for output, so the input budget is 7000. A system prompt costs 500 tokens. Process DOCS in the given order greedily: include a doc if current usage + its tokens <= 7000, otherwise skip it and keep checking later docs. Implement pack(docs, budget, system_tokens) returning (included_names_list, total_input_tokens_used). Print one line per doc in order: '<name>: included (<tokens> tokens)' or '<name>: skipped (would exceed budget)', then a final line 'input tokens used: <used> / 7000'. Print nothing else.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
CONTEXT_WINDOW = 8000
OUTPUT_RESERVE = 1000
INPUT_BUDGET = CONTEXT_WINDOW - OUTPUT_RESERVE  # 7000
SYSTEM_TOKENS = 500

DOCS = [
    ("doc1", 3000),
    ("doc2", 2500),
    ("doc3", 1500),
    ("doc4", 2000),
]

def pack(docs, budget, system_tokens):
    # TODO: start used at system_tokens; for each (name, tokens) include it
    #       only if used + tokens <= budget (greedy, skip-and-continue).
    #       Return (list_of_included_names, used_total).
    pass

# TODO: call pack, then print per-doc lines in DOCS order:
#   '<name>: included (<tokens> tokens)' or '<name>: skipped (would exceed budget)'
# TODO: print 'input tokens used: <used> / 7000'
practice_challenges.expected_output:
doc1: included (3000 tokens)
doc2: included (2500 tokens)
doc3: skipped (would exceed budget)
doc4: skipped (would exceed budget)
input tokens used: 6000 / 7000

en: Track used = system_tokens (500), then for each doc test used + tokens <= budget before adding.
Skipping doc3 does NOT stop the loop — doc4 still gets its own chance (it also fails here).

practice_challenges.test_code:
_inc, _used = pack(DOCS, INPUT_BUDGET, SYSTEM_TOKENS)
assert _inc == ["doc1", "doc2"]
assert _used == 6000
_inc2, _used2 = pack([("a", 6600), ("b", 100)], 7000, 500)
assert _inc2 == ["b"] and _used2 == 600
_inc4, _used4 = pack([("a", 6500)], 7000, 500)
assert _inc4 == ["a"] and _used4 == 7000
_inc3, _used3 = pack([], 7000, 500)
assert _inc3 == [] and _used3 == 500

en: Vertex AI's curated catalog of Google, partner (e.g. Claude), and open models with unified billing and governance

q.en: A high-volume ticket-classification workload should start on which Gemini tier?

en: The smallest/fastest tier (Flash-Lite class), upgrading only if evals fail
An open model on a TPU pod

q.en: Why must you reserve output tokens when packing context?

en: Output tokens are free but rate-limited
Input and output share the context window — no reserved room means truncated answers
The SDK refuses requests without a reservation

q.en: Which is a Vertex-specific advantage over calling a model with a personal API key?

en: IAM-scoped access, audit logging, VPC-SC perimeters, and provisioned throughput
